Customer is trying to deploy a PFX certificate to an iPhone enrolled into Intune. The trusted root certificate appears on the device but the PFX certificate does not.The connector logs show the following error: IssuePfx - COMException: System.Runtime.InteropServices.COMException (0x800706BA): CCertRequest::Submit: The RPC server is unavailable. 0x800706ba (WIN32: 1722 RPC_S_SERVER_UNAVAILABLE)
rootCause: Customer's PKCS profile in Intune had the following fields populated incorrectly: *Certification authority *Certification authority name

symptom: When trying to deploy a PFX certificate to an iPhone enrolled into Intune; the trusted root certificate appears on the device but the PFX certificate does not.
rootCause: The following error is seen in the connector logs: IssuePfx - The submission failed: Denied by Policy Module IssuePfx -Generic Exception: System.InvalidOperationException: IssuePfx - The submission failed at Microsoft.Management.Services.NdesConnector.MicrosoftCA.GetCertificate(PfxRequestDataStorage pfxRequestData, String containerName, String& certificate, String& password) Issuing Pfx certificate for Device ID xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx failed : Error is indicative of a failure on the CA. The machine account was not listed on the certificate template in the CA.
solution: Ensure that the machine account has access to the certificate template on the CA.

symptom: When you view the "Per-setting status" for a Device configuration profile you have deployed, you see a "SETTING" listed that you do not recognize, or cannot find in the Intune on Azure Portal anywhere underneath the "Settings" for the policy.An example is shown here: "AutomaticUpdateSchedule" In addition, this setting might report conflicts, remediation successful, or even errors. This is still an unknown setting and we do not know where to find it.
rootCause: It's very likely that this is by-design.When we migrated Configuration Policies to the Intune on Azure Portal, over from Silverlight, some settings were removed, due to no longer being valid.However, the setting is still part of the policy - you just cannot view it in the Intune on Azure Portal.As a result, policy evaluation still occurs - and settings might be reported as conflicts, or with errors - it's just possible that the setting is not visible in the Intune on Azure Portal, due to being deprecated. Since the policy is not present in the Intune on Azure Portal, we cannot display the information accurately.https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/intune/windows-update-for-business-configure#before-you-startThis article, and quote, are referencing Update settings - however, this may apply to other settings in the Silverlight Portal. Delete the update settings in the classic portal. After you migrate to the Azure portal and add the same settings to an update ring, you must delete the settings in the classic portal to avoid any potential policy conflicts. For example, when the same setting is configured with different values there is a conflict and no easy way to know because the setting configured in the classic portal does not display in the Azure portal.
solution: Per this article, this is by-design:https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/intune/windows-update-for-business-configure#before-you-startThere are [2] resolutions:1) If the issue is in regards to update settings, first you would add the same settings to an update ring. After they are configured and deployed, you should delete the update settings in the classic portal. 2) If you make a change in the migrated policy, from the Azure Portal, the settings should automatically removed from the policy.In addition, this will prevent editing of the policy - see the below screenshots.Before: "AutomaticUpdateSchedule" setting...After editing the policy in the Azure Portal (Simply changing a setting, saving the policy, and changing it back was enough) I now saw this.Verify the setting is disabled:

symptom: Due to the Intune on Azure migration, the feature of getting error codes from policies not working and apps not deploying on Intune managed devices is not accessible from the Azure console.The codes are still returned to the service, but these are not displayed.
solution: These codes can be retrieved using Graph Explorer:Log in with the tenant's administrator https://developer.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/graph-explorerAdd the following permissions: Recommend grabbing the policy GUID from the Azure console when selecting the policy you're trying to troubleshoot. For example:https://portal.azure.com/#blade/Microsoft_Intune_DeviceSettings/ConfigurationMenu/overview/configurationId/d32ef914-c0f2-4b16-915a-25c553cdbc97/policyJourneyState/The same result can be achieved with the following Graph query and grabbing the last character set from the policy you're looking for. You will need a Intune device id to view the policies mapped to it (my example is a Windows 10 computer).After grabbing the policy GUID, use the following query: https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/managedDevices/<UniqueDeviceId>/deviceConfigurationStates/<policyId>/settingStates

symptom: The Microsoft Intune Exchange Connector service is not able to start. When checking the system log in event viewer you see the following error message. og Name: SystemSource: Service Control ManagerDate: 10/23/2017 8:37:09 AMEvent ID: 7000Task Category: NoneLevel: ErrorKeywords: ClassicUser: N/AComputer: Server.domain.comDescription:The Microsoft Intune Exchange Connector Service service failed to start due to the following error: The service did not start due to a logon failure.Log Name: SystemSource: Service Control ManagerDate: 10/23/2017 8:37:09 AMEvent ID: 7041Task Category: NoneLevel: ErrorKeywords: ClassicUser: N/AComputer: Server.domain.comDescription:The WIEC service was unable to log on as .\WIEC_USER with the currently configured password due to the following error: Logon failure: the user has not been granted the requested logon type at this computer. Service: WIEC Domain and account: .\WIEC_USER This service account does not have the required user right "Log on as a service."
rootCause: This can be caused by the WIEC_User account does not have Log on as service permissions in the local policy.
solution: Assign "Log on as a service" to the service account WIEC_User on this computer. You can use Local Security Settings (Secpol.msc) to do this. If this computer is a node in a cluster, check that this user right is assigned to the Cluster service account on all nodes in the cluster. Here are some steps on how to do that on machine that has the Intune Exchange connector installed. 1. Click on Start and then search and type in either Local Security Policy or secpol.msc 2. In Security settings locate Local policy and expand it. 3. Then click on User Rights Assignment. 4. Locate "Log on as Service" and double click on it. 5. Click on Add User or Group and enter in WIEC_USER and click on OK. If you have already assigned this user right to the service account, and the user right appears to be removed, check with your domain administrator to find out if a Group Policy object associated with this node might be removing the right.

symptom: End users installing apps deployed from to their devices enrolled into Intune via Android for Work (AfW) receive error: "Your Administrator has not accepted permissions for this item."
rootCause: The specific app permissions have changed since the last time the Android for Work administrator approved them.
solution: The Intune/Android for Work administrator needs to log into the Android for Work admin portal at https://play.google.com/work, using the same account used to bind Android for Work to Intune, and re-approve the permissions for that app. After re-approving, end users should now be able to install the deployed apps.

symptom: Customer is having difficulty getting users to sync with IntuneTechnician wants to see clouduserID for users and/or see which users are licensed
rootCause: Useful for troubleshooting cloud user issues in SCCM Unified
solution: Open SQL Management Studio. Connect to the DB (customer should be able to help you choose the correct DB here)Once the database opens, Click on the "databases" folder, expand it out and find the "CM_XXX" folder.  Click that folder.  At the top, click "New Query"In the Query Window execute the following: (after typing in the command, hit the !Execute button at the top)To see all users: (IMPORTANT  - REPLACE CM_CM2 with the name of the database the customer is using, see step 2)select * from [CM_CM2].[dbo].[User_DISC]To see Specific Users (change '%testuser1%' to username@domain.com for the user you want to look up)select * from [CM_CM2].[dbo].[User_DISC] where User_Principal_Name0 like '%testuser1%' Once the results have been returned, look for the clouduser ID.  If no ID is found, the user isn't licensed to use Intune.  If it is, the user should be able to access and enroll devices/service with Intune/SCCM.  The ID is only assigned to users who have went through the cloud user sync process and matched users in the cloud.

symptom: Customer has an on-premise Exchange environment. Customer would like to know if you can block all 3rd party email apps, while allowing the Outlook and Native email app.No, the Outlook app does not support conditional access with on-premise Exchange. A conditional access policy to configure Exchange Active Sync is an all or none feature. For a hybrid Exchange environment, you can have Outlook with Conditional Access targeted to mailboxes that are Exchange Online mailboxes. The Outlook app does not support conditional access with Exchange on-premise mailboxes. Supportability for hybrid Exchange environments will depend on where the mailboxes reside since it specifically makes a difference for the feature availability and what support we can provide.

Customer has configured Intune on-premise Exchange conditional access.    The customer has some users that devices that are getting access to their email account without having to go through normal Intune  Exchange conditional access process.
rootCause: This issue can occur if the user has configured Exchange active sync device rule.  An on premise Exchange server activesync device rule can override Intune on premise Exchange conditional access action.
solution: To find the users that are having are bypassing the Intune on-premise Exchange Conditional access you can run the following command in the Exchange command shell Exchange 2013 higher Get-Mobiledevice  -mailbox user@smtpdomain.com | ft DeviceID, DeviceAccessState, DeviceAccessStateReason Exchange 2010Get-Activesyncdevice -mailbox user@smtpdomain.com | ft DeviceID, DeviceAccessState, DeviceAccessStateReason You will get results like these DeviceId                                            FriendlyName                             DeviceAccessState       DeviceAccessStateReason --------                                               ------------                              -----------------       -----------------------SEC144C96FE594E7                              heroltexx                                      Blocked                    Individualandroidc350666989                              Nexus 9                                        Allowed                    Individual79C4MQU9U16C9DEQRUSMFAKQU8  iPad mini 2                                   Allowed                    DeviceRuleIn the results if you look at the device access state reason it will be set to DeviceRule.    When the device access state is set to device rule that means that the device is allowed or blocked because of Exchange activesync device access rue.      To resolve the issue you will need to remove the Exchange ActiveSync Device access rule.     You can check on the rule by running the following command in the Exchange command shell. Get-ActiveysncDeviceAccessRule     To resolve the issue you can remove the ActiveSync device access rule.    When  you remove the Exchange ActiveSync  device access rule the users where granted will be blocked.     If you don't want the user to be blocked  access to there  email.   You can add  users the DeviceID to use ActiveysncAllowedDeviceIDs.  Tis can be done by running the following command in the Exchange Command Shell. Set-CasMailbox user@smtpdoman.com -ActivesyncAllowedDeviceIds    <Device ID > Here is example of the set-casmailbox command in action.  Set-CASMailbox user@smtpdomain.com  -ActiveSyncAllowedDeviceIDs 79C4MQU9U16C9DEQRUSMFAKQU

symptom: A Windows 10 PC enrolled is being marked as non-compliant when a Compliance Policy is set to require BitLocker and Encryption, yet both are enabled on the PC.
rootCause: The Require Encryption and Require BitLocker requirements are not met until BitLocker encryption is completed.  Based on factors including disk size, the amount of files, and BitLocker settings the completion of encryption make take a long period of time.
solution: From an Intune perspective, this behavior is By Design.  A device will be marked as compliant once the encryption completes.

symptom: Customer cannot manage iOS and Android devices in Intune for Education portal(http://intuneeducation.portal.azure.com)
rootCause: By Design.  Intune for Education manages Windows 10 devices.  However, customers that have Intune for Education also have access to the full version of Intune for managing other mobile device platforms.
solution: Follow the Intune documentation for managing iOS and Android devices: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/intune/device-management

symptom: Activation Lock State is showing as "Not enabled."Other fields are showing accurately, including "Supervised" as yes.
rootCause: DEP enrollment is not directly related to configuring devices for Activation Lock Bypass. There are 2 configurations required:iOS Supervised mode enabled Enabled on the DEP enrollment profile � OR Enabled as option on Apple Configurator preparationTargeted with Device Configuration profile explicitly allowing Activation Lock to be used on supervised devices.
solution: Unless both of these are present on the device, the Activation Lock will automatically be ignored on MDM reset � no bypass needed. If the pre-requisites listed above were met in the customer environment, so inconsistency is coming from the Bypass service from Apple.

symptom:
Users are able to access corporate email on third-party mail apps when targeted by MAM-CA (app-based Conditional Access) Policies, which only
allow the Outlook App to authenticate to Exchange.***For this scenario, Native EAS Client is being quarantined so we know the policy is working**
rootCause: A cause for this is the third-party app is using the EWS (Exchange Web Services) protocol.Known Apps that use EWS1. Edison Mail app for iOS and Android2. Newton Mail app for iOS and Android
solution:
EWS is not currently supported for MAM-CA policies. A possible workaround, as EWS is legacy authentication, is to block apps that do not use modern auth: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/intune/app-modern-authentication-block
The best way to tell if an app is using EWS Protocol, is to run a Fiddler trace.

symptom:
OverviewIt is important to have a basic understanding of CSPs (Configuration Service
Providers) in order to monitor and Troubleshoot CSPs for Windows 10 Personal
Computers (PCs).  This article does not cover Mobile Devices but could be used in supplement of Mobile Devices by utilizing
this write up with the articles under Reference Articles sub sections for
Mobile Windows Phones.



What is a CSP:

A CSP is an
interface in the client operating system between configuration settings
specified in a provisioning document and configuration settings on the device.
Their function is similar to that of Group Policy client-side extensions in
that they provide an interface to read, set, modify, or delete configuration
settings for a given feature. Typically, these settings map to registry keys,
files or permissions. Some of these settings are configurable and some are
read-only.

 

Starting in Windows
Mobile 5.0, CSPs were used to manage Windows mobile devices. In the Windows 10
platform, the management approach for both desktop and mobile devices
converges, taking advantage of the same CSPs to configure and manage all
devices running Windows 10. Each CSP provides
access to specific settings. For example, the Wi-Fi CSP contains the settings
to create a Wi-Fi profile.

 

CSPs are behind many
of the management tasks and policies for Windows 10 in Microsoft Intune and
non-Microsoft MDM service providers. For example, in Intune, the policy to
allow search suggestions in the Microsoft Edge address bar uses
Browser/AllowSearchSuggestionsinAddressBar in the Policy CSP.  CSPs receive
configuration policies in the XML-based SyncML format pushed to it from an
MDM-compliant management server such as Microsoft Intune. Traditional
enterprise management systems, such as System Center Configuration Manager, can
also target CSPs by using a client-side WMI-to-CSP bridge.

 

 

Why should you learn
about CSPs:

Generally,
enterprises rely on Group Policy or MDM to configure and manage devices. For
devices running Windows, MDM services use CSPs to configure your devices.

 

In addition, you may
have unmanaged devices, or a large number of devices that you want to configure
before enrolling them in management, or you want to apply custom settings that
aren't available through your MDM service. The CSP documentation can help you
understand the settings that can be configured or queried.

 

CSPs in MDM:

Most, if not all,
CSPs are surfaced through your MDM service. If you see a CSP that provides a
capability that you want to make use of and cannot find that capability in your
MDM service, contact your MDM provider for assistance. It might simply be named
differently than you expected. You can see the CSPs supported by MDM in the
Configuration service provider reference.

 

When a CSP is
available but is not explicitly included in your MDM solution, you may be able
to make use of the CSP by using OMA-URI settings. In Intune, for example, you
can use custom policy settings to deploy settings. Intune documents a partial
list of settings that you can enter in the OMA-URI Settings section of a custom
policy, if your MDM service provides that extension. You'll notice that the
list doesn't explain the meanings of the allowed and default values, so use the
CSP reference documentation to locate that information.

 

(Quoted from: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/configuration/provisioning-packages/how-it-pros-can-use-configuration-service-providers)

 Troubleshooting CSPs

[How to get error information on a CSP]

One of the first
ways you can see the status for an Profile \ CSP Custom Profile deployment in
the Azure Intune Portal is by navigating to Microsoft Intune->Device
Configuration->Assignment Status

 

Here you can see the
following information:

- Display Name

- Type

- Devices with
Errors

- Devices Failed

- Devices Succeeded

 

Currently there is
no documentation that provides a breakdown of Devices with Errors, Devices
Failed or the ability to click on these and get further detail.

So where does this
leave you with getting the information you need to understand why there are 1
or maybe 6 devices showing with Errors, and what are these errors?!?

The first place I
recommend getting further data for CSP Custom Profile deployments would be by
going to Microsoft Intune->Device Configuration->Profiles.

Once there you will
want to select the policy that is showing under the "Microsoft
Intune->Device Configuration->Assignment Status" as having devices
with errors or devices failed.

After you select that
policy, and the blade opens, you will be able to see data on the Overview tab
right away, but that is not our focus. 

If you deployed the
Profile to a device, move to the Device Status section under Monitor. Here you
will see the following information:

- Device

- User

- Deployment status

 

This shows you the
device, the user assigned to it and their deployment status (more detail than
just 1 failed)

If you deployed the
Profile to a user, move to the User Status section under Monitor. Here you will
see the following information:

- User

- Device count

- Deployment Status

- Last Check-In

 

This shows you the
UPN, the number of devices they have with their deployment status (more detail
than just 1 failed)

If you wish to see
the status information for the CSP's them self-based on a per-setting status as
some times its not the profile that fails but a setting in the profile itself,
you can select the Per-Settings status section under Monitor. Here you will see
the following information:

- Setting

- Description

- Remediation

- Conflict 

- Unknown

- Error

- Not applicable

 

Using these sections
under Monitor per each of the Profiles you have created should provide you
further information on what is going on from a console, stand point. However,
if you are wanting further detail for those errors that are truly now that you
have identified what device, user or setting that is the issue at play from
these tools, you will want to move to the client device its self to start
troubleshooting.

 

[Methods to  troubleshoot a CSP]

When troubleshooting
Windows 10 Custom Profile OMA-UIs, or Windows 10 Policies themselves, one of
the first things you want to get used to doing is Collecting the logs directly
from Windows 10 PCs.

Making sure you get
the most out of these logs is also important, so be sure to follow the steps
after enabling "Show Analytic and Debug" log options, to do this
follow these steps:

 

1. Open the Event
Viewer

2. Click Viewer

3. Choose Show
Analytic and Debug logs options

 

Now that you have
the debug enabled and can get some serious meat on these logs, you want to
start collecting that data for analysis for investigation on your CSP failures.

When it comes to
CSPs you want to get the DeviceManagement-Enterprise-Diagnostic-Provider event
logs, you can do this following these steps:

 

First Navigate to
Applications and Services Logs > Microsoft > Windows >
DeviceManagement-Enterprise-Diagnostic-Provider

 

To collect Admin
logs:

a. Right click on
the Admin node.

b. Select Save all
events as.

c. Choose a location
and enter a filename.

d. Click Save.

e. Choose Display
information for these languages and then select English.

f. Click Ok.

 

To collect Debug
logs:

a. Right click on
the Debug node.

b. Select Save all
events as.

c. Choose a location
and enter a filename.

d. Click Save.

e. Choose Display
information for these languages and then select English.

f. Click Ok.

 

Now the Admin
Channel logs events for the OMADM Client which is built into Windows 10, by
default which processes the OMA-UIs, but for detailed logs the Debut Logs
contain rich logging of events.

Once you have these
collected you are ready to start reviewing them for details on the errors you
came to find, to utilize for the status messages you found in the Azure Portal.

 

Now there is another
set of Event Logs you would want to collect, when troubleshooting CSP, these
are the Azure Active Directory Logs.

These can show if
you are getting issues with communicating with AAD, Authenticating, Workplace
join issues... all of which could affect you getting a CSP Profile to the
device.

To collect these
follow these steps:

 

First Navigate to
Applications and Services Logs->Microsoft->Windows->AAD

 

To collect Analytic
logs:

a. Right click on
the Analytic node.

b. Select Save all
events as.

c. Choose a location
and enter a filename.

d. Click Save.

e. Choose Display
information for these languages and then select English.

f. Click Ok.

 

To collect
Operational logs:

a. Right click on
the Operational node.

b. Select Save all
events as.

c. Choose a location
and enter a filename.

d. Click Save.

e. Choose Display
information for these languages and then select English.

f. Click Ok.

 

Now you can also
review these to sort out deeper detail and data on your console errors to help bring resolution.

If you find that the
data you have gathered is still not getting you the end result or resolving the
issue you are facing.

I would advise
opening a technical support case with Microsoft as we have additional tools
like ETW Traces and the ability to look at SyncML files from the back end.

This allows us to
aid you further in your investigation, be sure to include all of the steps
taken, screen shots from Monitoring data and also your event logs exported.

THIS CONTENT IS NO LONGER UP TO DATE. FOR THE MOST UP-TO-DATE CONTENT, REFER TO THE INTUNE CSS WIKI FOUND HERE: SAW Actions - Overview (visualstudio.com) This article covers the steps necessary to perform an MDM authority reset from Configuration Manager to Intune.  Note:  The MDM reset procedure will set the tenant authority to Intune Standalone. It is not possible to reset the authority to Unknown, SCCM, or, Office 365.
solution: Steps below to be completed by Flighting team members Note: The steps below are to be completed by the Intune SAW team only, you will need to be a member of group IntuneSupport-SAW-Users | Permission Level: ReadWrite in RAMWEB in order to execute flighting requests in Jarvis (Geneva).    Accept the MDM reset collab in DFM as appropriate.  Validations Confirm that the following is true: Scenario requires MDM authority reset per VKB 2834797 DFM case contains valid Global Admin approval Refer to Intune: What qualifies as a valid Global Administrator or Intune Administrator approval for a SAW request? (microsoft.com) Search Assist365 with the tenant domain that was confirmed by GA and confirm that the data matches the tenant data that was provided by the case owner Execution Once you confirmed the above, proceed as follows: Go to https://portal.microsoftgeneva.com/actions Set Environment to Public. Click Go to Geneva Actions. Complete authentication prompts. On the left select Microsoft Intune/ Identity & Subscriptions/ MDM Authority Reset Workflow Request. In the middle pane update the fields as follows:Endpoint: <AMSU>ContextId: <Context ID>Domain: <default domain> (this could be any domain that has been verified with the tenant)   Click Get Access.Enter 187261959 as the Work Item Id.Hit Submit to request JIT access. Note: All JIT requests are manually approved. You will be cc-ed on the Pending JIT access notification that is sent to the approvers. If there is no approval within 1 hour, reply all to the notification and ask for approval.   Once approved, double check the tenant data and complete authentication prompts.   Click Run.   Verify completion:On the left select Microsoft Intune/ Identity & Subscriptions/ Search WorkFlow Provider.Click Run to view past workflows for previously selected tenant.The reset is considered complete when the output for the performed MDM reset contains the following:ProcessingState : 2 -> The Workflow has completedCompletionPercentage : 100   Refresh Assist365 and confirm that the tenant now shows MDM authority Intune. Notify engineer that MDM reset is completed successfully Update your notes. Add an entry to this ICM https://portal.microsofticm.com/imp/v3/incidents/details/187261959/home with the following format:DFM 1234567  | MDM Reset Close the AR.

symptom: This article describes how to find the UPN and display name for a user when all you have is the user ID and the name of the tenant. This can be useful when reviewing logs files and the only reference to an event is the UserID property.

Is there a way to have applications install automatically on enrolled HoloLens instead of having to manually install from Company Portal or from Microsoft Business Store?Yes. Confirm that the following has been set:  1. Ensure that Intune is set up as a management tool for WSFB2. In the apps section of Intune, set the License Type to "User licensing" and the Type to "Required"
When the customer has to update the certificate for the Cisco ISE App Registration in portal.azure.com, the steps provided are only listed for the classic portal
solution: a. Open the Azure Active Directory Blade and then open "App Registration"    b. Click on the Cisco ISE application and click the "Pencil" (Edit) icon to make changes to the               Manifest file    c. Open Powershell, and run the below script in order to get the changes to the manifest (Note: You         must have the path for the cert to input next to the command)        $cer = New-Object System.Security.Cryptography.X509Certificates.X509Certificate2        $cer.Import(c:\temp\isecert.cer)        $bin = $cer.GetRawCertData()        $base64Value = [System.Convert]::ToBase64String($bin)        $bin = $cer.GetCertHash()        $base64Thumbprint = [System.Convert]::ToBase64String($bin)        $keyid = [System.Guid]::NewGuid().ToString()        echo $keyid        echo $base64Value        echo $base64Thumbprint    d. Once the script ran, we took the components needed in order to update the "Manifest"    e. The customer updated the keyiD, the base 64Thumbprint, the expiration date of the cert, and the         start date of the cert. Once the changes were made,               the customer was able to save the update

symptom: Customer reports that on iOS 11.x when sending a corporate file from a managed app to another they noted they can see non-managed apps in the list of applications presented.
rootCause: This is expected behavior for iOS 11.x that has been confirmed by the Product Group. Apple has removed the hooks to filter out non-managed apps from the share sheet so Intune cannot pre-filter out apps like in iOS 10.x releases.
solution: This is now expected behavior, please note to the customer that Intune encrypts files before handing off so only Managed Apps have the ability to retrieve the encrypted file and unencrypt them.If an application that is non-managed attempts to access the files they will receive errors like the examples below:[Example 1][Example 2]

rootCause: This is due to a change made by apple restricting requirements around the application transport security: https://blogs.technet.microsoft.com/intunesupport/2017/02/25/meeting-apples-new-requirements-for-application-transport-security/The product group has confirmed that the Preview release of the Intune Company Portal for MacOS does not include the bits to compensate for this.
solution: The RTM release of the Intune Company Portal for MacOS will contains the correction for this and should be coming out by end of 2017.

symptom: Customer attempts to configure Azure Intune portal settings, example Enrollment Restrictions->iOS changing Block to Allow for even a GA UPN is met me with error "An error occurred while saving Request ID: ########-####-####-####-############".
rootCause: This can be caused when a Proxy is in the environment and is not configured for the URL Whitelist to include the Intune items for proper communication. While this error can be caused by multiple items this tends to be one of the most common reasons and should be validated before escalation.
solution: Provide the customer the following URLs for the Proxy Whitelist: *.windowsazure.com *.azure.com *.*.core.windows.net (storage access, etc., but needs to allow two levels; e.g., *.blob.core.windows.net, *.table.core..., *.queue..., *.file...). *.cloudapp.net *.azurewebsites.net *.database.windows.net *.trafficmanager.net *.office.com Then have them retest the configuration change.

symptom: The information below describes the steps to take when a customer is requesting to change their MDM authority from Office365 to Intune, or from Intune to Office365
rootCause: It is not possible to reset the MDM authority when switching between O365 and Intune
solution: Co-Existence is the supported method for changing between Office 365 and Intune MDM authority. This is a feature customers can enable for themselves, Escalations will not normally be needed to enable Co-Existence between Office 365 MDM and Intune.  Here are the scenarios and how we handle each one:SDK 4495350: How to enable MDM for O365 as an additional authority when tenant authority is Microsoft Intune SDK 4502550: How to enable Intune as an additional authority when tenant authority is MDM for O365Note: If it is not possible to activate coexistence per above SDK articles, validate that the customer has an active Office 365 subscription that includes Exchange Online (if the goal is to add MDM for O365 as an additional authority), or, an active subscription for Intune ,EMS, or M365 (if the goal is to add Intune as an additional authority), and, that the logged on user is a global administrator. If prerequisites are fulfilled, but it is still not possible to activate coexistence via the UI, then please engage the flighting team per 3090501.

symptom: Intune Managed Browser FAQStarting on January, 27, 2020, Intune will no longer support the Intune Managed Browser.Starting on February 1, 2020, the Intune Managed Browser will no longer be available in the Google Play Store or the iOS App Store. At this point, you will still be able to target new app protection policies to the Intune Managed Browser, though new users will not be able to download the Intune Managed Browser app. In addition, on iOS, new web clips that are pushed down to MDM-enrolled device will open in Microsoft Edge instead of the Intune Managed Browser.On March, 31 2020, the Intune Managed Browser will be removed from the Azure console. This means you will no longer be able to create new policies for the Intune Managed Browser. If you have existing Intune Managed Browser policies in place, they will not be affected. The Intune Managed Browser will show up in the console as an LOB app with no icon, and existing policies will show as targeted to the app still. At this point, we will also remove the option to redirect web content to the Intune Managed Browser within the Data Protection section of App protection policies.Public link: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/intune/fundamentals/in-development#take-action-use-microsoft-edge-for-your-protected-intune-browser-experienceMS Internal OnlyThis information is to be considered Microsoft Confidential and this content is not approved for sharing with customersThe purpose of this content is to inform our CSS Support team of how to answer Frequently Asked Questions and provide faster FDR on known issues Native application for iOS and Android device Available on the Apple App Store and Google Play Store. Available in China on Baidu, Huawei, Tencent, Wandoujia, and Xiaomi Integrated with the Intune SDK Enable protected web browsing. Other apps managed by Intune can be configured to send web traffic to the Managed Browser The Managed Browser be configured to restrict which sites users can navigate to Support for Intune Conditional Access Support for general web browsing, Tabbed Browsing, bookmarks Support for single sign-on via myapps.Microsoft.com Built on standard system webview (UIWebView for iOS, and Android WebView) All codecs/scripting languages/HTML support is identical to the support provided by UIWebView on iOS and WebView on Android Android WebView: https://developer.chrome.com/multidevice/webview/overviewiOS UIWebView: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uiwebviewPlugins not supportedNo monitoring or manual interference in network trafficOnly impact to browsing is *before* http(s) calls are initiatedExamples: URL Allow/Block list, Azure AD Application Proxy automatic redirectionBoth iOS and Android Managed Browser allow use without Application Protection Policies. If another app on the user's device has enrolled in Intune and retrieved MAM policy, the Managed Browser automatically enrolls and gets policy (assuming the admin has targeted it for policy)Per-app and device-wide VPNThe Managed Browser does not behave differently than any other application targeted by per-app VPN. This initiates a VPN session for all Managed Browser traffic.Intune and the Microsoft Edge browserEdge supports Intune App Protection policies and now supports the same feature set It is the replacement for the Intune managed browser.

Intune receives the data regarding MDM enrolled devices that exceed usage quotas from DatAlert, however, Intune does not disable data roaming on the device.  User also shows up as "Unknown" in the DatAlert web page.
rootCause: Device is enrolled into Intune as an Android for Work device. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/intune/telecom-expenses-monitor shows what is supported
solution: Android For Work does not have access to the Samsung KNOX APIs that perform this scenario. It will work for normal Android Samsung device, but not when enrolled as Android For Work.

symptom: Intune Device RemovalBrowse to https://portal.azure.com/#blade/Microsoft_Intune_Devices/DeviceEntryBlade/mDMDevices Login with your Org ID or Work account with GA rights.This will show you a list of Intune Device records for any device that has contacted the Intune service in the last 120 days. Click the device you want to remove and click 'Remove Company Data'If the device already shows 'Retire Pending' or 'Retire Issued' and you are comfortable that corporate data has been removed from device. Then click the 'Delete' optionThe device entry should be removed in about 15 minutes but in some cases you may need to close the browser and reopen Azure AD RemovalYou may also want to remove the device record from "Azure AD Devices' link or by clicking here https://portal.azure.com/#blade/Microsoft_Intune_Devices/DeviceEntryBlade/aADDevicesThis is a list of device records stored with Azure Active Directory and may be separate records from Intune Device records. The records aren't marked for last updated time and could be tied to the "Work Account" on the device itself. If you remove the record the user might have to add their work account back to the deviceTo proceed with device record deletion you would click on the device and click 'Delete'
Devices targeted by compliance policy, showing in both the Device Compliance and Devices without compliance policy reports in the Azure Intune Portal under Microsoft Intune->Device Compliance.
rootCause:
This is due to a mis-configuration of the Compliance Policy AssignmentAs a result the devices are not actually getting a Compliance Policy ran against them.The device current default behavior is to state the device is then Compliant, so it shows as such in the UI.However if you run the following Kusto Query against the Device ID, you will verify there is no Compliance Policy being applied. 

CalculationQueueLibrary

| where EventId ==
40991

| where deviceId ==
"06e5ab8e-fd4a-46ba-b514-ccf71ed0ba2e"

| summarize
argmax(env_time, complianceState, complianceDetails) by complianceState

This behavior however is going to change at the end of 2017, where the default behavior will change.
solution: Issue resolved with Intune 1803 service release, see https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/intune/device-compliance-get-started

symptom: Unable to enroll device due to message, Error:[DeviceTypeNotSupported] during the enrollment process via the Company Portal site or app.
rootCause: Using Kusto, confirm the following query against the UserID to get the enrollment attempt and more information on the error itself:EnrollmentStatusByUserID('<UserID>',datetime(2017-11-07 14:00),datetime(2017-11-10 14:00),1000)Should return messages that looks like this:2017-11-09 13:35:16.6727886 f600f765-66e3-4fa4-ac40-928e9c67d448 83b65487-5669-4df0-ad8b-2ae3ee47c86d 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000 27e86607-8761-4c06-b3c2-1cb1acfddce7 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000 IntunePE  BYOD IPhone ClientFailure EnrollmentCriteriaNotMet Criteria Not Met Criteria Not Met DeviceLifecyleUsing Kusto, follow that query with one more to see if you are able to see a history of this:EnrollmentHistoryByUserID('<UserID>',datetime(2017-11-07 14:00),datetime(2017-11-10 14:00),1000)Should return messages that looks like this:2017-11-09 13:38:39.0888987 83b65487-5669-4df0-ad8b-2ae3ee47c86d 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000 27e86607-8761-4c06-b3c2-1cb1acfddce7 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000 46804 EnrollmentAddDeviceFailedEvent       IPhone UserPersonal      EnrollmentCriteriaNotMet Criteria Not MetBoth queries show that there is a Criteria not being met which is causing the device to fail enrollment.This is caused by an Enrollment Restriction mis-configuration.
solution:
To correct this you need to validate the Enrollment Restrictions for the tenant.You do this by navigating to: Microsoft
Intune->Device Enrollment->Enrollment Restrictions->Device Type
Restrictions->Default->Platform
Configurations Here you can assure that the Min and Max settings are correct for the platforms you wish to support for enrollment.Example, if a customer wants to allow only iOS devices that are a minimum of 11.0.3 to enroll to their tenant but no maximum, they would do the following:1. Set the iOS Versions for Min to 11.0.3 and the Max to default2. SaveExample: Just remember if you set the maximum on these that you will be back to bump it up at some point, best practice would be to set min but only set max if you know a release you do not want to allow to enroll is up and coming.Reference article: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/intune/enrollment-restrictions-set

symptom: DEP enrolling device, iOS 11.0.0 and newer no longer shows Restore from iCloud option during Setup Assistance.The enrollment profile for your iOS DEP devices shows that the Restore is set to Show, yet the Restore from iCloud option is still missing.Example from Portal: The same enrollment profile works fine with iOS 10.0. devices all the way up to iOS 11.0.0 but after that it stops showing.
rootCause: This is due to a iOS 11.0.0 and newer feature change by Apple, they now have an extra requirement to allow restore from iCloud backups.They now require the Apple ID to be entered at the time of the restore as a security check, so if the Apple ID is not shown as part of the Apple DEP enrollment process, the feature restore from iCloud is not shown.Example on device:
solution: Changing the Enrollment Profile to so that the Setup Assistant will show the Apple ID on the device will allow the restore from iCloud feature to show again.Example from Portal:Example on device:

rootCause: Wi-Fi profiles using certificates are not coming down to Android devices with the certificate referenced in the profile.
solution: Issue has been resolved

symptom: Customer tries to edit the security group associated with Intune in the Sandblast MTD console under Settings -> Device Management -> Advanced Settings -> Edit Settings but the following error appears: "Warning security group "xxx" is missing from MDM"
rootCause: Group is not listed in Active Directory
solution: Group must be in Active Directory before being added to the Sandblast console.

symptom: Customer is using Sandblast Mobile Threat Protection with Intune.  Customer has configured a compliance policy that marks the device as non-compliant if a risky app is detected by SandBlast Mobile and a conditional access policy to block access to Outlook if the device is out of compliance. SandBlast Mobile detects the risky app but does not block access to Outlook.  Conditional access will prevent the user from getting further mail but does not stop access to mail that is already on the device.
rootCause: By design.  When a user initially enters their PIN an access token is granted and Intune doesn't control the token expiration. That's an AD function. Azure AD would have to re-evaluate the token and deny access.
solution: By design. This will only work in instances where the token has not been issued or has expired. and the risky app is detected.

symptom: The customer wants to automatically enroll local domain joined PC's into Intune using Azure AD Automatic Enrollment for Windows 10 devices, however when trying to enroll a Windows 10 Pro 1607/1703 device that is joined to a local domain, the device only gets Azure AD Registered and not MDM enrolled. This is a hybrid Azure AD joined device scenario.
rootCause: This can occur if hybrid Azure AD joined device setup is not configured. You must first configure Azure AD Hybrid in order for this scenario to work.
solution: First, the client computers must be running at least Windows version 1709. Next, complete the steps to configure AAD hybrid setup at https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/device-management-hybrid-azuread-joined-devices-setup.Once the configuration is complete, the user can then click "Connect" on the PC using a local admin account. It is important that the device is not domain joined prior to doing this.Once the admin joins the computer to the domain, they can then log off and have the regular user log in. The device should be automatically enrolled into MDM.

Users report that �Per APP VPN� doesn�t work anymore on iOS devices because the certificates have expired. The certificates were initially deployed via a per app VPN profile. Users usually discover this when opening the app configured for �Per APP VPN� and recognizing that the app is failing to access data.
rootCause: This occurs because the certificates fail to automatically renew. In my case this was ICM 32775 (See https://icm.ad.msft.net/imp/v3/incidents/details/32775183/home).
solution: The workaround is to create a new SCEP profile and accompanying VPN/Wifi profile. See 117091816352445 for more details.Related cases include:117040715572384117022294104190116122115093167

symptom:
�        
The �Windows Defender Security Center� is
showing unexpected results on Windows 10 1703/1709

In 1709, it will show everything as disabled (see below screenshot as an
example) despite settings being present.

o  


�        
You can confirm that Windows Defender Settings
deployed via Microosft Intune are present on the device, despite this interface
not showing up correctly. 

o  
Run PowerShell as an Administrator

o  
Run the command: �Get-MpPreference� and compare
the settings displayed via what is shown from thecommand output. 

o  
Confirm
that the variable: UILockdown is set to (True) 

o  


 

If the value of UILockdown
is set to �True�, and the
customer has the setting �Enable user
access to Windows Defender� is set to Allowed,
then this article shows a workaround for this
rootCause:
The �Enable user access to Windows Defender� is not working
correctly. Setting it to �Allow� actually prevents
the access to Windows Defender, by FORCING the UILockdown to be enabled.

The �Enable user access to Windows Defender� setting
modifies a CSP (Policy CSP > Defender > AllowUserUIAccess) and incorrectly
sets it.
solution:
The workaround, until a fix is implemented to resolve the
CSP being set incorrectly, can be found here.


 On
     your existing Windows 10 Configuration Policy that you�re deploying, change
     the �Windows Defender Antivirus > End-user access to Defender� to �Not
     Configured.� Save the current policy. 
 
  This alone will not fix the issue � setting it to �Not
      Configured� means the existing setting is simply not changed� eg; if the
      UILockdown is already enabled, it will stay enabled. However � it ensures
      new PC�s do not receive the setting to block the UI for Defender. 
 
 Create
     a new, custom setting to correctly enable User Access. 
 
  The setting we are overriding, is the Windows CSP
      (Configuration Service Point) that Intune sets via the UI (The UI sets it
      incorrectly, so manually setting it bypasses that)
  This setting is found here: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/client-management/mdm/policy-csp-defender#defender-allowuseruiaccess
      
  In Microsoft Intune, go to� 
  
   Device Configuration 
   Profiles
   Create profile
   Name: AllowUserUIAccess (you can name it what you want)
   Platform: Windows 10 and later
   Profile type: Custom
   Settings > Configure
   �Add�
   Name: AllowUIUserAccess
   OMA-URI: ./Vendor/MSFT/Policy/Config/Defender/AllowUserUIAccess
   Data type: Integer
   Value: 1
   Create & deploy the profile to the groups you
       specify. 
   
  
 


 

Once this custom setting is defined, deploy it to the
security groups of your choice.

Verification the policy worked: 

�        
View the Windows Defender Security Center� if
everything shows up fine and as expected, you know it works. 

�        
Alternatively, run the �Get-MpPreference� command, and view the variable of the �UILockdown�


o  
You can also run: Get-MpPreference | FL UILockdown and verify the variable is False. 

o

You can easily locate an Collection ID and location \ path of a collection in a Intune Hybrid SCCM Console that is lost or there are too many nested collections to walk through with the following process:1. Open SQL Studio2. Expand DB3. Expand CM DB4. Select Table - right click and select Filter settings5. Filter for collection and click OK6. Seelct Collections_G, right click and show top 10007. Find the CollectionName in the table, then find the Collection ID will be sitecodexxxxxx (Note: The returned data is a little mis-leading the CollectionID column is not actually the true CollectionID you want its the SiteID column examples below)8. Open a new query9. Run query: select FolderPath, InstanceKey from Folders a inner join FolderMembers b on a.ContainerNodeID = b.ContainerNodeID where InstanceKey = 'COLLECTIONIDHERE' 10. execute[Example]Here you see a example of a nested collection, while in this example its simple to find imaging a customer with hundreds of nested collections.Using the steps above we see the following results:Taking this collection ID, we perform the query to see the following results: As you can see you get the path of the collection so you can easily locate and navigate inside the console to the collection.
